result:
The Museum of Unfinished Things

I first noticed the building on a Tuesday that felt like a Thursday, one of those dehydrated afternoons when the sky forgets its trade and the minutes clump together like wet socks. The structure had arrived overnight, wedged between the shuttered travel agency and the pawn shop that only accepted childhood memories. Its façade was the color of unanswered e-mail, and the brass plaque beside the door read, in letters that seemed to rearrange themselves when I blinked: THE MUSEUM OF UNFINISHED THINGS. 

Underneath, in smaller type: ADMISSION FREE WITH A SAMPLE OF YOUR OWN INCOMPLETENESS.

I reached into my pocket and produced, without surprise, the guitar pick I’d bought in 1997 when I intended to learn “Blackbird.” The guard—a woman whose lanyard identified her as Docent of Abandoned Cadenzas—accepted the pick, bit it to verify authenticity, and waved me through. The turnstile clicked like a metronone missing every third beat.

Inside, the lobby’s ceiling was painted with the constellations of half-remembered passwords. I tilted my head and recognized Orion’s belt as the login to my first Hotmail account. Someone behind me whispered the security question to my college loan portal; the words tasted like cafeteria soup. A sign pointed to the coat check, but the hangers were all shaped like question marks, so I kept my jacket.

The map described four floors: Almost, Not Quite, Maybe Later, and the unlit basement simply labeled “—” I took the stairs because the elevator required a floor number I hadn’t imagined yet.

Almost was arranged like a greenhouse of sapling novels. Manuscripts grew out of terracotta pots, their stems green with envy, sprouting pages instead of leaves. Some had wilted in the middle of a sentence—“. . . she opened the envelope and inside was”—the final word replaced by a small, brown aphid. Docents in aprons labeled “Developmental Editor” walked the rows with watering cans full of red ink. One invited me to prune a thriller that had grown into a hedge maze; I clipped off four adverbs and a subplot about a parrot who knew Morse code. The plant sighed, leaves fluttering into a crisper outline. The editor gave me a sticker that said “Plot Doctor, First Class,” but it peeled off my jacket like it had second thoughts.

At the far end stood a bonsai epic fantasy trilogy. The first book was full-grown, the second a stunted twin, the third a seed the author had swallowed during a panic attack. A tiny sign warned: DO NOT ASK ABOUT THE MAP. I knelt, anyway, and discovered the continent unfinished: mountain ranges labeled “here be dragons, no really, I mean it,” rivers that ran off the edge into white space, and a capital city whose name was a placeholder pronounced *mumble*. When I breathed on the parchment-colored bark, the ink scent carried memories of 3 a.m. and too much caffeine gum. I felt the gravitational tug of my own file back home—folder titled “Great American Something,” last opened 2012.

A rope made of red pen ink separated visitors from the exhibit of almost-relationships. Glass cases displayed half-knit scarves still waiting for winter to matter, text-message threads frozen mid-typing (“I was going to say . . .”), and wedding cakes with only the first tier iced, plastic bride and groom locked in side-eye. One pedestal held a voicemail that materialized as a soap bubble; when I leaned in, it whispered, “It’s not your fault, it’s—” then popped against my cheek, leaving the taste of closure that wasn’t mine.

Not Quite was darker, lit by the blue glow of loading bars. The walls were padded with buffering symbols that throbbed like jellyfish. Here lived the almost-inventions: the electric toothbrush that also recorded dreams (patent pending since 1998); the umbrella that forecast the weather by playing appropriate jazz; the universal remote that could mute other people’s children. Each prototype wore a sticker reading 99% COMPLETE, FLAW IS COSMIC. A hologram of the inventor of a perpetual motion snooze button explained that the device worked perfectly except for its requirement that the user remain asleep while pressing it. He laughed the laugh of someone who has shareholder meetings in nightmares.

In the center, suspended by cables, hung the architectural model of a city where every crosswalk leads to the sidewalk you daydreamed about instead of the one you were headed toward. Tiny model citizens stood forever mid-stride, one foot in ambition, one in obligation. The plaque noted the project stalled when engineers realized the sewer system would have to carry unfinished thoughts, and no pipe had been invented that wide.

I watched a child press her face against the rail. She wore a T-shirt printed with the Fibonacci sequence in Comic Sans—either a prodigy or a warning. “Mom, why doesn’t it work?” she asked. Her mother, holding a stroller containing only the idea of a sibling, replied, “Because grown-ups forget what they started for.” The girl nodded solemnly, then began to climb over the barrier. A guard shaped like a download icon stepped forward, but she had already inserted her hand into the model. Streets wrapped around her wrist like warm taffy. She pulled out a miniature traffic light blinking yellow in all directions. “For my collection,” she explained. No one stopped her; the museum runs on barter.

Maybe Later felt like a waiting room upholstered in twilight. Rows of plastic chairs faced a screen that displayed the next episode thumbnail but never buffered. Here were the abandoned online courses: Intermediate Finnish, watercolor for people who can’t stop apologizing, mindfulness for the perpetually early. Certificates of completion hovered just above each station, printed on fog. When I exhaled toward one, my breath became module 7 of 12. I inhaled and learned three Finnish cases, then immediately mispronounced my own name in a Nordic accent.

A doorway curtained with calendar pages led to the Hall of Rescheduled Demises. Death itself had procrastinated here: a man who’d postponed his heart attack by telling the EMT he’d “catch it next time,” a plague postponed by rats who unionized, a galaxy whose heat death had been tabled due to lack of quorum. Each story ended mid-sentence at the moment of deferral. Visitors lingered longest before a small display labeled My Own, though the case was empty except for a Post-it: Back in five minutes—D. I touched the glass and felt my pulse stall, curious, before deciding to grab a snack first.

Descending the spiral staircase, I lost the concept of footfalls; each step absorbed the sound like unpaid invoices. The basement level had no ceiling, only the underside of the floor above, pipes labeled with names of people who ghosted therapists. The air tasted like the pause before an apology. Here, finally, was the gift shop.

No postcards, only blank rectangles labeled “Wish you were—” The pens provided ran out of ink halfway through signatures. I tried to purchase a snow globe containing a manuscript caught in a blizzard of red corrective marks, but the cashier rang it up as “potential” and asked if I wanted to open a line of credit with my remaining ambition. I declined; the interest rate was dreams plus 23%. 

Instead, I traded the memory of my seventh birthday party for a reusable shopping bag stitched from first drafts. It wriggled with sentences that still believed they’d be novels. As I left, the exit turnstile requested not the guitar pick but something larger. I offered the cashier my fear of completing tasks; she weighed it on a scale calibrated to childhood measurements. It came to 2.3 pounds, just under the limit. She wrapped it in yesterday’s weather and handed me receipt paper that began to fade before I finished reading: Thank you for visiting the Museum of Unfinished Things. Please come back whenever you are ready to leave.

Outside, dusk had reorganized the street. The travel agency was gone; in its place, a popup store selling futures at half-price. The pawn shop now accepted only completed symphonies. I walked home carrying the reusable bag, inside it the soft rustle of paragraphs attempting to conjugate the verb “to finish.”

That night I opened a document titled “Museum Visit.” I typed the first sentence, then stopped to look up the exact shade of “unanswered e-mail.” While the browser wheeled its chromatic circle, I noticed the guitar pick on my desk—returned, impossible. I held it between thumb and forefinger, felt the tremolo of every song I never learned, and began again.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
